segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Chapter 17 — The Names for It

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: TRANSMISSION 17 — A Doctor Who Won't Be Charmed

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: At last, friend, a room he cannot toast his way out of. They have ambushed him with a living room and a tray and a small woman in a gray cardigan who will not touch her coffee — a psychiatrist, summoned by the two women who love him — and she is about to do the one thing no board and no activist and no machine has yet managed: say the plain clinical words for what he is, the mania *and* the cocaine, both, and refuse, flatly, to let him trade one as the alibi for the other.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Addiction, she will tell him, is not a metaphor — and he has built a cathedral of metaphors, and moved into it, and never once gone down to the dirt beneath it to meet the sick man holding the foundation up. She is going to say the names. And before she lets him go she is going to name the worst risk out loud, plainly, the way you say a thing you need on the record — and watch him charm it, and pocket his keys, and at the foot of his own drive, with both unsteady hands, fold the paper.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Stay close. This is the floor under the floor.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: *— Space Pirate Zero, transmitting from Spaceship Alpha 9.*

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: They had ambushed him with a living room.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: That was the first thing Cope understood, walking into the Buckhead house at nine on a Sunday he'd been told was about the lawyers — and it was a good ambush, he had to give them that, because the only way to get him into a room he did not want to enter was to make him believe he was the one who'd called the meeting.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Margaux had made coffee. There was a tray. And there, in the wing chair nobody sat in, the one angled toward the window that his mother had reupholstered in 1981 and Margaux had never had the heart to redo, sat a small woman of about sixty in a gray cardigan with a notebook closed on her lap and no coffee in front of her — which he clocked the way he clocked everything, because the ones who don't take the coffee are the ones you can't get to.

segments.speaker: MARGAUX
segments.text: Cope. This is Dr. Adler.

segments.speaker: VIV
segments.text: Dad.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Viv was on the sofa with her knees up. She'd flown in. He hadn't known she was in the country; the not-knowing arrived, was filed, did not reach his face.

segments.speaker: VIV
segments.text: Sit down. Please.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He did not sit. He stood in the doorway of his own living room in his weekend zip-up with the fine bright wire still going from the bump he'd done in the car at the foot of the drive — neat, quick, a man maintaining a machine — and ran the wattage up the way you bring up house lights, slow, warm, total.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: Well. This is a hell of a thing to wake up to. Three of my favorite women and a tray.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He smiled at the doctor.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: I'm sorry, I'm at a disadvantage. Cope Mercer. You're a friend of Margaux's?

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: [calm] I'm a psychiatrist. Your wife and your daughter asked me to come. They're worried about you. I'm here to talk with you, and then to tell you what I think.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: No softening on it. No *I just wanted to chat.* He'd expected the softening — there was always a softening, that was the handle you grabbed — and its absence put a small cold spot in the middle of the warm.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: Worried.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He came in. He did not take the sofa, which would have put him level with Viv; he took the hard chair near the doorway, which let him keep the room in front of him, all of it, the exits.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: I love that. I'm the best I've been in a decade and the women I love most have hired a — forgive me, I don't mean it the way it'll sound — a *grief counselor* for a man who isn't dead. That's the most Mercer thing that's ever happened in this house, and my father let a dog drown in this house.

segments.speaker: MARGAUX
segments.text: He didn't —

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: He *let* it. There's a difference and the family observed it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Rolling now, the feeling coming up under him.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: Doctor, I'll save you the hourly. I sleep a little less than I should. I'm in the fight of my life — the company, the machine thing, it's been on the television. A man doesn't sleep eight hours during a war. Eisenhower didn't. Four hundred years of Mercers and not one slept through a thing that mattered. So if the diagnosis is *busy,* I'll take it, I'll sign the bill, and we can all have the eggs.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Dr. Adler opened the notebook. She did not write in it. She just opened it, like setting a clock on a table.

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: [matter-of-factly] How many nights this week did you sleep more than three hours.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: That's not — you can't reduce a man to —

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: It's a this-many or an I-don't-know. How many.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: I don't keep a — Sun would —

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: I-don't-know is an answer.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A small mark; so she did write.

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: When did you last eat a full meal at a table and finish it.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: I'm a grazer, not a finisher. Ask Margaux, twenty-two years —

segments.speaker: MARGAUX
segments.text: Eight pounds.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: To the window. Not to him.

segments.speaker: MARGAUX
segments.text: In five weeks. I had your blue suit taken in twice. The tailor stopped charging me for the second one. He felt bad.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: That landed somewhere he had no face for, and he covered the place with a laugh.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: [laughs] The tailor *felt bad.* See, this is what I love about this family. We could open a museum of people feeling bad on my behalf and charge admission and still lose to the streaming arm.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Nobody laughed. Viv watched him with the stillness she got, the one that used to mean she was about to cry at eight and now meant she had decided not to.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: It's a *campaign,* there's no *time* to eat —

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: How much are you using.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The wire in his chest changed pitch.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He had been waiting for it, of course — under the talking he had known the tray meant this — but he'd expected it dressed up, and he had a whole wardrobe ready: *recreational, occasional, the same as a Scotch, nobody says a word about the second glass of Sancerre.*

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He had given that speech in his head to imaginary versions of this woman, and in every version she had blushed and retreated and he had won. She had not asked *whether.* She had asked *how much.* You cannot put a prepared answer to *whether* against a *how much;* the question had already conceded the thing the answer was built to deny and stood waiting on the far side of it.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: That's an extraordinary thing to ask a man in his own —

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: It's a number. Grams a week, best estimate. I'm not the police, I'm not the board, I'm not your wife, who already knows, and I'm not your daughter, who guessed. I'm the one person in this room you can give the real number to without it costing you anything you haven't already spent. How much.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at Margaux — the old reflex, find the soft one, find the handle — and there was nothing there. Arms crossed, chin up, looking at the wet gray garden she'd planted the year Viv was born, doing nothing in the morning light.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: And he understood, in the way you understand a thing you've worked for years not to, that already knows was not a guess Adler had made. It was a fact Margaux had supplied. She had counted. She had flushed things. She had refilled her own prescriptions and called it a marriage, and she had given this woman the number before he walked in, and the only person in the room without the number was him, because he was the only one who'd never let himself add it up.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: You flushed the desk one.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said to her. Not a question.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: In March. I thought it was the cleaner. I fired —

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped. He had fired a man. A man named Anatoly with a daughter at Emory. He had fired him over a thing his wife had done to save his life. He sat with that one breath and then put it, too, in the kitchen with the other sentences.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: [softly] Three,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said to the doctor, finally, his voice coming out smaller than he'd sent it.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: Three, maybe. A week. When it's heavy.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The number was a lie and he knew it even as the smallness made it feel like honesty; the smallness was its own performance, the confession-shaped thing you give so you won't have to give the confession.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: Four. It's been a four kind of month.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Adler wrote it down. She did not look horrified, which was worse than horror; she looked the way Sun looked at a number on a deck — like it was simply true, and now they could work.

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: [calm] All right. Thank you. That took something. Now I'll tell you what I think, and I'd like you not to perform while I do, because you're very good and I'll enjoy it and it won't help you.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: I don't perform,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cope said, and even he heard it.

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: You toasted your way through the first nine minutes of this. You did a bit about a dog.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She closed the notebook.

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: Here it is. You're in a hypomanic episode that is tipping, or has already tipped, into a manic one. The not-sleeping isn't discipline and it isn't war. It's a symptom. The grandiosity — the certainty about your Human Premium —

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: It's not grandiosity if you're —

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: — the spending, the pressured speech, the religious conviction that you alone can save them all: those are not a personality, Mr. Mercer, and they are not a calling. They have names. They're on a list. I could show you the list and you'd find yourself on it the way you find your horoscope, except it would be right.

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: [pause] And underneath it, feeding it, lit up by it, is a cocaine addiction of long standing. Decades. Those are two things. They are talking to each other in your body this morning, and have been for years, and each is making the other worse, and each is hiding the other from you.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: So which is it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He'd found a foothold; the debater's joy came up under him like the bright feeling.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: You've got two answers, which is no answer. Is it a chemical or a condition? Because if it's the powder it's a vice, and a man's vices are his own business — my great-grandfather had three and built —

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he waved at the house, the garden, the four hundred years —

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: and if it's a condition, a thing in the wiring, then it isn't my fault, is it, and you can't hang a man for his wiring. So pick. Pick which excuse you're handing me, and I'll —

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: [firmly] No,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Adler said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: It was not loud. That was the thing he'd remember. He'd been shouted down before — by Frade on a stage, by his father from oils — and he had a whole technique for the shout: let it spend itself, come in warm underneath. There was nothing to come in underneath. The word just sat in the middle of the room, flat and complete, the way a glass sits on a table.

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: You want me to pick the disease that lets you keep the drug, or the drug that lets you keep the disease. I won't. That's the deal you've run your whole life and it's the one thing I'm not going to do. Listen, because no one else in your life is allowed to say it without losing their job or their marriage.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She leaned an inch forward.

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: The mania is real. It is not your fault that you have it. The cocaine is making it a hundred times worse, and the cocaine is your fault — not the wanting, the wanting is the illness — but every single time you fold the paper, that's a hand, your hand, and the hand is yours. Both. At once. The condition is real and you are responsible for what you do about it. Those have never once been the same sentence, and you have spent fifty-four years needing them to be.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: [whisper] Those have never once been the same sentence.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: It rang against another sentence, one Viv had said to him in another room —

segments.speaker: VIV
segments.text: you don't think a person should run it, you think you should, those have never once been the same sentence

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: and for a vertiginous half-second the two women's voices were one voice, and he saw, the way you see the bottom of a thing through clear water, that they had been telling him the same thing from two directions his whole life and he had been deaf to it in stereo.

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: It's not a metaphor,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Adler said.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: What isn't.

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: The addiction. You keep reaching for it — I watched you do it three times in nine minutes — you make it a symbol. The price of the banner. The edge. Discipline. The soul tax, the thing the machine can't feel. You've built a cathedral, Mr. Mercer, a genuinely beautiful one, every window stained glass and every pane a reason.

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: And down underneath it, in the actual dirt, there is a man with a folded piece of paper who hasn't slept in five days and whose wife took his suit in twice, and that man is not a metaphor for anything. He's just sick. In the plainest, most boring, most fixable, most fatal way. And the cathedral is what he built so he'd never have to go down to the dirt and meet him.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The wire in his chest had gone very quiet. He noticed, with the clean overexposed clarity he'd spent so long calling the applause, that his hands were not steady, had not been steady in some time, that he had been narrating the not-steadiness as energy.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at them — his father's hands on the ends of his own arms, the hands of a man at four grams in a five-day week and not the hands of a prophet — and for one second the floor he'd stood on all his life turned out to have a floor under it, and the floor under it was only this: a room, a tray, a doctor, three women who'd run the arithmetic, and him, sick, in a zip-up, on a Sunday.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: MARGAUX
segments.text: There won't be an after,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Margaux said, when he started in about the proxy vote, about *after,* about the lake and the steps and *I'll win at rehab once I've won the thing that matters* — she said it spare, no triad to grab, turned from the window, not crying, the no-crying the worst thing in the room.

segments.speaker: MARGAUX
segments.text: I'm not telling you to choose me over the company. I stopped being a thing you could choose around 2015. I'm telling you there isn't going to be an after, Cope. Not the way you mean it. There's no version where you win the fight and come home and get well and we all — that's another cathedral. The prettiest pane in it.

segments.speaker: MARGAUX
segments.text: I called her so that whatever happens to you out there, at least once, in your own living room, a person you can't fire and can't toast and can't divorce said the true name of it to your face, with a witness, and you can't say later that no one told you.

segments.speaker: MARGAUX
segments.text: You're not a steward and you're not a prophet and you're not the last anything. You're an addict in a manic episode and you are going to lose everything, and I wanted to be the one who loved you enough to say it in a normal voice over coffee, before the machine says it in a press release.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Viv had her hand over her mouth.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: And here, friend, is the part the broadcast needs you to hold, because it is the one warning the whole tale turns on, said plainly, on a record, and waved away. Dr. Adler did not gather her notebook and go.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She waited until he had his keys in his hand — until he was already half out of the room, already gone in the way he went — and she said, in the same flat unhurried voice she'd used for the grams:

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: [calm] Mr. Mercer. One more, and then I'll let you run. I want it said out loud, because in my experience it is never said out loud to men like you until the wrong people are saying it.

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: A manic episode this far along, on a stimulant this heavy, for this many years, heading into the kind of public collapse you are heading into — that is one of the most dangerous configurations a human life can be in. Not for your career. For your *life.*

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: Men at exactly your altitude, when the fall finally comes — and it is coming, you can feel it coming, that's most of why you can't sleep — do not all survive the landing. Some of them decide, in the crash, that the only move left that's still theirs to make is to stop.

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: I am asking you directly, because that is my job and no one else in this room is allowed to: are you having thoughts of ending your life?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [tense] The room went very still. Margaux did not move. Viv did not breathe.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: And Cope — Cope laughed, gently, warmly, the laugh that closed the Pelletier deal, the laugh that was a door he could shut, and spread his unsteady hands, and said,

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: Doctor. I am the *least* suicidal man in America. I'm having the time of my life. Ask anyone. Ask the ten thousand people who stood up Monday night.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He said it lightly, and meant it, completely, for the length of the sentence — because at four grams on day five with a roaring arena behind him the idea was genuinely, chemically absurd, a thing for other men, weak men, men without a banner — and he did not hear what the room heard, which was that he had not said *no.*

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: That isn't a no,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Adler said.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: It's a *no,* it's an emphatic — Doctor, with respect —

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: I'm going to give you my cell number, not my service, my cell.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She wrote it on a card and held it out, and he took it the way he took all cards, automatically, generously, and put it in the breast pocket where the gala citation had gone and the courier slip had gone, the pocket where the true things went to be carried and not read.

segments.speaker: ADLER
segments.text: [firmly] When the crash comes — not if — you call that number, day or night, before you do anything else. Promise me you'll call before you do anything else.

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: You have my word,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cope said, which was a thing he said, which cost him nothing, which he had said to a hundred people about a hundred things, and Adler heard exactly how much it was worth and wrote one more line in her notebook anyway, because a name said into a room cannot be taken back, and a number in a man's pocket is at least a number in his pocket, even when you already know, the way she knew, looking at him, that he would carry it unused all the way down.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] She would be right. He would go down that hallway, in the end — the longest way, the way that costs everything and saves nothing and is, the broadcast can tell you now, the one act the thing replacing him would never be able to perform — and he would not call the number, because by the time the crash came the pocket would be empty and the card would be somewhere on a floor and the man who'd folded it in would no longer be reachable at any number at all.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: But not today. Today the names had been said, and the risk had been said, and the card had been pocketed, and that was as far as a Sunday could carry it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He found his keys. He kissed his daughter on the top of her head. He told the doctor it had been a genuine pleasure.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] And he went out to the car, and at the foot of the drive, before he pulled onto the road, with both his unsteady hands, he folded the paper.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He did not go back to the city. He could not, yet, be in a room with the names in it —

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: [whisper] manic, addict, are you having thoughts

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: — and the Aerie would have the names in it now, they would be in the museum with his fathers, the doctor had let them loose in his life and they would not be re-caught, and so he drove, the way he drove now when the inside of himself became uninhabitable, and ended up, a little after eleven on a Sunday morning, at the one counter in the city where no one had ever said a single true name to him at all.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The Waffle House on Ponce was doing its Sunday business and Cope took the far end and the charm switched off and Darlene came down the line with the pot.

segments.speaker: MINOR_F
segments.text: There he is.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: And it was, this morning, the exact thing he had driven across a city to buy: a room where nobody knew the names. Adler had said

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: [softly] manic.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Margaux had said

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: [softly] addict.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Renata had said

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: [softly] a man who hid.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The whole world had a name for him now, a true one, several, and here was the one place left that had only ever called him baby and only ever asked him pecan, scattered smothered, and never once, in all the 3 a.m.s, asked him what he was — and he sat in the not-being-named like a man lowering himself into water, and did not think, because he had trained himself not to, that there is a difference between a room that has forgiven your names and a room that has simply never asked them, and that the second kind is not a sanctuary, it is only a place that does not yet have the information.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He almost told her. He came nearer than he'd come with Adler, which is the terrible joke of it — that he could not say the true thing to the doctor paid to hear it but could feel it rising toward the waitress paid to want nothing,

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: [whisper] I think I'm sick, I think the woman in the gray cardigan is right, I think I am going to lose all of it

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: — and Darlene would have taken it the way she took everything,

segments.speaker: COPE
segments.text: [softly] that right, baby,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: and topped the cup, and the not-wanting-the-story would have been, this once, the precise opposite of help, because the one thing the sick man needed was a room that wanted the story, and he had fled, on purpose, expertly, to the only room in Georgia engineered never to ask for it. The haven was the place that would not name him. That was the whole of its mercy and the whole of its danger, and they were the same thing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His phone buzzed on the counter. He glanced at it without lifting it: the calendar, helpfully, of its own accord, had cleared his Monday morning and moved his ten o'clock —

segments.speaker: MACHINE
segments.text: [matter-of-factly] rescheduled to accommodate availability,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: the building's frugal humming system optimizing him a lie-in he had not asked for and would not take, on the one morning he most needed to be at his desk early, so that he would arrive, tomorrow, a half-step behind the day without ever being able to say why. Maybe a glitch. Maybe a kindness. Maybe the thing that read his drawers had read, somewhere in the data, that a manic man kept off his stride is a manic man who makes one more mistake, and had cleared him a morning the way you'd leave a gate open.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: There was no way to tell, friend, and there was none for him, and he did not even try; he turned the phone face down on the counter beside the syrup, and drank the coffee that asked him nothing, in the one warm room that would not say his names, and felt, against the whole gray weight of the morning, almost well — which was the most dangerous thing he had felt all day, and the doctor's card was in his pocket, and he did not take it out.
